models.name: BERT
models.tagline: Built to understand language, not generate it.
models.description: Google's 2018 breakthrough. Still the backbone of most production NLP classification pipelines today.

architecture.insight: BERT reads the entire sentence at once, in both directions simultaneously. This bidirectional context makes it exceptional at understanding — but it fundamentally cannot generate new text. It has no mechanism for predicting "what comes next."
architecture.parameters: BERT-base has 12 transformer layers, 12 attention heads, and 110M parameters. BERT-large scales to 24 layers, 16 heads, and 340M parameters.

training.title: BERT · Masked LM + Next Sentence Prediction
training.objective: Fill in the blank, predict the pair

steps.title: Masking
steps.text: 15% of input tokens are randomly replaced with [MASK]. The model must predict the original token using only surrounding context.

steps.title: Bidirectional context
steps.text: Because BERT sees the whole sentence at once, both left and right context inform every prediction — unlike earlier left-to-right models.

steps.title: Next Sentence Prediction (NSP)
steps.text: Given two sentences, BERT also learns to predict whether sentence B naturally follows sentence A — building paragraph-level understanding.

steps.title: No labels needed
steps.text: Both tasks are self-supervised — the labels come directly from the text itself, enabling training on billions of tokens without any human annotation.

yes: You need to classify, tag, or extract from text
You want a small, fast, production-ready model
You're building semantic search or embeddings
Your task is understanding, not generating
no: You need to generate text or have a conversation
You need long-form reasoning or complex answers

models.name: Claude
models.tagline: Safety-first assistant built to reason and converse.
models.description: Anthropic's safety-first assistant. Built to reason, converse, and follow complex instructions — with alignment baked into its training through Constitutional AI.

architecture.specs: Causal · RLHF + Constitutional AI · 200K context · Proprietary

layers.label: Causal (masked) self-attention
layers.detail: each token sees only what came before

layers.label: RLHF + Constitutional AI alignment
layers.detail: trained to be helpful, harmless, honest

architecture.insight: Claude is a decoder — it generates text one token at a time. What makes it distinctive isn't the base architecture (similar to GPT-style models) but the training: Constitutional AI bakes in values and safety through a self-critique process, not just human feedback labels.
architecture.parameters: Claude uses rotary positional embeddings (RoPE) enabling up to 200K token context. Training pipeline: pretraining → SFT → RLHF → Constitutional AI critique-revision loops.

training.title: Claude · Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback + Constitutional AI
training.objective: Learn from humans, then from principles

yes: You need safe, reliable, long-context reasoning
You're building a product via API without hosting
Safety and alignment are critical requirements
You need complex instruction-following or analysis
no: You need full model access or custom fine-tuning

models.name: LLaMA
models.tagline: Open-weight foundation for the open-source LLM ecosystem.
models.description: Meta's open-weight model family. The foundation of the open-source LLM ecosystem — designed to be studied, fine-tuned, and deployed privately.

layers.label: Grouped-query attention (GQA)
layers.detail: efficient — fewer KV heads than Q heads

architecture.insight: LLaMA is architecturally similar to GPT but optimised for efficiency and open use. Grouped-query attention dramatically reduces memory during inference. SwiGLU activations improve performance. The result: competitive with much larger models at a fraction of the compute cost.
architecture.parameters: LLaMA 3 (2024) ranges from 8B to 405B parameters. The 8B model runs on a single consumer GPU. Context window: 128K tokens. Weights are publicly released — download, inspect, modify, fine-tune on your own hardware.

training.title: LLaMA · Next-token prediction · 15 trillion tokens
training.objective: Predict what comes next, at massive scale

steps.title: Causal LM objective
steps.text: Given a sequence of tokens, predict the next one. Simple, but applied over 15 trillion tokens of diverse text — books, code, web, scientific papers.

steps.title: Efficiency first
steps.text: LLaMA was designed to be maximally capable within a given compute budget — smaller models trained on more data, informed by the Chinchilla scaling laws.

steps.title: Open weights
steps.text: Meta releases the trained weights publicly. The community fine-tunes them with instruction datasets (Alpaca, ShareGPT) to create instruction-following variants.

steps.title: No alignment by default
steps.text: Base LLaMA is a raw language model. Safety and alignment are the responsibility of whoever fine-tunes it — a deliberate choice enabling research freedom.

yes: You want open weights for full control
You're fine-tuning on a custom domain or dataset
You're doing research or building open-source tools
no: You lack GPU resources for self-hosting

guideSections.label: they're structurally built — encoder, decoder, attention, layers
they behave differently — masked LM, RLHF, Constitutional AI
to reach for each — benchmarks, code, decision guide

insights.0: "The" attends most to itself — but also tracks the second "the" as a related article.
insights.1: "animal" strongly attends to "it" — BERT resolves this coreference by reading both left and right context simultaneously.
insights.2: "didn't" spreads its negation influence across neighboring tokens.
insights.4: The second "the" groups with "street" — articles naturally cluster with their nouns.
insights.5: "street" attends to "cross" and "the" — it's the object of the verb phrase.
insights.6: "because" bridges both clauses, attending in both directions.
insights.7: "it" strongly attends to "animal" — correct pronoun resolution using full bidirectional context. A causal model might have picked "street" instead.
insights.8: "was" links "it" and "tired" as a copular verb.
insights.9: "tired" completes the predicate, attending to "it" (subject) and "was".

rlhfSteps.label: Pretraining
rlhfSteps.desc: Train on a massive corpus of text to build general language understanding and generation capability — the same as any large language model.

rlhfSteps.label: Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT)
rlhfSteps.desc: Fine-tune on human-written examples of ideal responses — helpful, harmless, honest conversations across a wide variety of tasks.

rlhfSteps.label: Reward Model Training
rlhfSteps.desc: Human raters compare pairs of model outputs and rank them. These preferences train a separate reward model that scores response quality.

rlhfSteps.label: Reinforcement Learning (PPO)
rlhfSteps.desc: Use the reward model to fine-tune the LLM via Proximal Policy Optimisation — maximising the reward signal while staying close to the SFT policy.

rlhfSteps.label: Constitutional AI (Anthropic-specific)
rlhfSteps.desc: The model critiques and revises its own outputs against a written set of principles — reducing reliance on human feedback for safety, and making the values transparent and auditable.

constitutionalPrinciples: Choose the response that is least likely to contain harmful or unethical content.
Choose the response that is most honest and acknowledges uncertainty where it exists.
Choose the response that is most helpful to the human, given that it is also safe and ethical.
Choose the response that a thoughtful senior Anthropic employee would consider most appropriate.

options.label: Generate text or have a conversation

steps.question: Do you need to self-host or fine-tune on private data?

r-bert.title: Use BERT
r-bert.body: Your task is about understanding, labelling, or extracting from text — not generating it. BERT's bidirectional encoder excels here: sentiment analysis, NER, document classification, semantic search, extractive QA. It's small, fast, and proven in production. Fine-tune a pre-trained checkpoint on HuggingFace in minutes.

r-claude.title: Use Claude
r-claude.body: You need high-quality generation, reasoning, or instruction-following — and you're comfortable calling an API. Claude's Constitutional AI training makes it reliable and safe out-of-the-box, with a 200K token context window ideal for long documents, code review, complex analysis, and research assistance.

r-llama.title: Use LLaMA
r-llama.body: You want open weights — the ability to inspect, fine-tune, and deploy the model entirely on your own infrastructure. LLaMA 3 (8B–405B) runs on consumer or cloud hardware. Ideal for domain-specific chatbots, RAG pipelines, research experiments, and any product where data privacy or customisation are non-negotiable.

qaChips: What makes BERT better than LLaMA for classification tasks?
How does Constitutional AI make Claude different from other LLMs?
Can I fine-tune Claude like I can fine-tune LLaMA?
